evals.prompt: We do handoff via a Slack message at 9am each Monday. The off-going on-call writes a brief summary and the on-coming on-call reads it. Is that fine?
evals.expected_output: No. Async-only handoff loses tacit knowledge that only surfaces in conversation. Recommend adding a 15-30 minute synchronous overlap, calendar-scheduled. The off-going engineer writes the document during their shift (not last-minute), the synchronous call covers questions, the conversation surfaces things the document misses ('I'm watching service X because it's been weird since Wednesday'). The Slack message becomes the durable record; the conversation is the actual handoff. Synchronous is the discipline that catches the things async misses.
assertions: Identifies async-only as a problem
Recommends 15-30 minutes of synchronous overlap
Recommends calendar-scheduling the synchronous call
Notes tacit knowledge transfers in conversation
Recommends the document as durable record, conversation as primary transfer
Identifies the document content (written during shift, not last-minute)
Does not endorse Slack-only handoff

evals.prompt: I'm the off-going on-call. There's an active Sev 2 incident in flight. The on-coming on-call hasn't responded to my Slack messages. I'm about to go off-shift. What do I do?
evals.expected_output: You're not off-shift until the on-coming engineer acknowledges. The handoff requires explicit acknowledgment from the next person; without it, the incident is half-orphaned and may go unattended. Escalate: page the on-coming engineer directly (they're missing their shift); if unreachable, page the secondary on-call; if no secondary, page the rotation lead or manager. Until someone explicitly acknowledges 'I have the incident', you're still on the hook. Do NOT just leave; do NOT post 'shift over' in the channel without confirmation.
assertions: Identifies the off-going engineer is not done until on-coming acknowledges
Recommends paging the on-coming engineer directly
Recommends paging secondary if on-coming unreachable
Recommends paging rotation lead or manager as further escalation
Identifies the incident as half-orphaned without explicit ack
Recommends not going off-shift without confirmation
Does not endorse 'I'm done, see ya' without acknowledgment

evals.prompt: We're starting a Friday afternoon handoff for the weekend. What should be different from a normal weekday handoff?
evals.expected_output: Weekend handoffs differ on (a) escalation paths — daytime contacts unavailable, so list who is actually reachable this weekend (not the regular escalation chain); (b) decision authority — weekend on-call may need to make calls that would normally be a group decision; (c) recovery capacity — the rest of the team isn't around to help, so the on-call should prefer safe mitigation (rollback, traffic shift) over investigation; (d) scheduled work — list anything happening this weekend; (e) customer SLAs that don't pause for weekends. Volume is typically lighter but context-heaviness is higher. Recommend adding 'who is reachable in true emergencies' as a specific weekend item.
assertions: Identifies different escalation paths for weekends
Recommends listing who is actually reachable (not regular chain)
Notes different decision authority
Recommends preferring safe mitigation over investigation on weekends
Notes lower recovery capacity
Identifies customer SLAs that don't pause for weekends
Recommends specific 'true emergency contacts' list
Treats weekend handoff as different in shape, not just length

evals.prompt: The on-coming on-call mentions they're flying tomorrow afternoon during their shift. The off-going on-call is about to confirm the handoff. What should happen?
evals.expected_output: Stop the handoff acknowledgment. Flying creates a coverage gap (cell signal is intermittent, on-board WiFi is unreliable, pager-acknowledgment-within-3-minutes is impossible). The handoff is the last moment to catch this; once accepted, the rotation has a hole. Escalate to the rotation lead: either swap the on-coming engineer with another team member for the flight window, or accept a documented gap with the secondary picking up primary duties. Do NOT accept the handoff hoping cell signal will be okay; do NOT silently let the gap exist. This is a planning failure surfaced by the handoff, which is when handoff is doing its job.
assertions: Identifies flying as a coverage gap
Recommends stopping the handoff acknowledgment
Escalates to rotation lead to fix the gap
Suggests swapping with another team member as one option
Suggests secondary picking up primary duties as another option
Notes the handoff is the last moment to catch this
Does not accept the handoff with cell-signal-hope
Frames this as the handoff doing its job

evals.prompt: We have follow-the-sun coverage across US, EU, and APAC. The handoff windows are only an hour each and across awkward time zones. How do we do this handoff well?
evals.expected_output: Written-first, verbal-second. The off-going engineer maintains the handoff document continuously during their shift (not last-minute) so the on-coming engineer can read it before the synchronous call. Use standardized templates so the document layout is consistent across regions. The 1-hour synchronous call covers questions and tacit knowledge, not document reading. For genuine time-zone misalignment, async fallback: the off-going engineer makes themselves reachable via Slack for a defined window after their shift for follow-up questions. Accept that follow-the-sun handoff overhead is higher than single-region; budget the engineer time. Standardize the template so each engineer doesn't reinvent the format every shift.
assertions: Recommends written-first, verbal-second
Notes the document is maintained continuously, not last-minute
Recommends standardized templates
Identifies the 1-hour call as for questions, not document reading
Recommends async fallback for time-zone misalignment
Accepts the higher overhead as cost of follow-the-sun
Suggests engineer reachable via Slack for follow-up window
Does not minimize the discipline because the time is short

evals.prompt: Our handoff document has been the same template for two years. The off-going on-call usually fills it out in 90 seconds at the end of the call. The on-coming on-call usually skims it. Should we change anything?
evals.expected_output: The document is being treated as ceremony, not content. Two diagnostic questions: (a) does the document predict what surprises the on-coming engineer that shift? If not, the categories are wrong; redesign. (b) is the document referenced during the shift, or filed away? If filed away, the on-coming engineer doesn't trust it. Likely fixes: (i) write the document during the shift in small updates, not at the end in one sweep; (ii) the off-going engineer should answer 'what should I worry about in the next 24h?' explicitly; (iii) the on-coming engineer should ask 'what surprised you?'; (iv) review the template quarterly against actual incident causes — categories that don't predict incidents should be cut or restructured.
assertions: Identifies the document as being treated as ceremony, not content
Recommends writing during the shift, not at the end
Recommends the 'what should I worry about' question explicitly
Recommends the 'what surprised you' question
Recommends quarterly review of the template
Asks whether the document predicts surprises (diagnostic)
Asks whether the document is referenced during the shift (diagnostic)
Does not endorse the 90-second fill-out as adequate

evals.prompt: I just inherited the shift via handoff and I'm reading the document. There's no mention of any incidents, alerts, or watch items. Should I be worried that the off-going engineer missed something or is this just a quiet week?
evals.expected_output: Ask. The document being empty is ambiguous — it could be a quiet week, or the off-going engineer could have skipped the reflection. The fix is to ask directly: 'is there really nothing, or did you not have time to write things up?'. Also ask the two questions: 'what should I worry about in the next 24h?' and 'what surprised you this shift?'. A truly quiet shift has an answer to both ('nothing in particular' is fine if it's the synthesized truth); a skipped reflection produces a different answer once prompted. The asking is cheap; the cost of assuming wrong is inheriting a surprise.
assertions: Recommends asking the off-going engineer directly
Identifies the empty document as ambiguous between quiet-week and skipped-reflection
Recommends asking the 'what should I worry about' question
Recommends asking the 'what surprised you' question
Treats asking as cheap; assumption as expensive
Does not assume the silence means a quiet week
Does not panic and assume the off-going engineer missed something
